speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May 6th, 1996. The survivor is Sydney Schwimmer, and this is tape number six. What I'd like to go into now is to establish the dates, the time of your deportation from Dolha to the ghetto and then your ghetto, the transport then from the ghetto to Auschwitz. So when were you and your family transported to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a day after the Passover, I believe it could have been around 16 or 17 of April 1944. Well, this Dr. Batory somehow arranged that in Dolha there were two or three typhoid cases. And the gendarmes were afraid very much to transport Jews or anyone else because there was something like was too dangerous. So our town, Dolha, we arranged we stay with a nice man, the Dr. Batory. There maybe had been some diseases but not typhoid. And these gendarmes or whoever were the-- in charge of this postponed, that this couldn't be transported. Dolha couldn't be evacuated, couldn't be deported, because on account of typhoid. They were probably giving, having these orders also not to transport. They were very much afraid that the typhoid will spread. And so Dolha remained for the next two weeks being still in Dolha. But we knew for sure that someday they'll come and transport us. They take us too from our house. There was a man by the name of Wolf Klein in our town, a very smart man. And I consulted the same [INAUDIBLE] let's escape. Let's go someplace. He had some friends there, because he was a wealthy man, and he was in the food business. He had non-Jews who could have hidden him. And he wanted with me also. And I said, let's do it, both of us, because something will happen here, and-- But he had a wife and several children, and he was very well known. And I was also known to gendarmes. And I figured out like this, if I do something like this, they're going to torture my father. It's going to be some big troubles. They're going to torture his wife and the children. We knew it in advance.

speaker: interviewee
text: So next day somehow we couldn't make up our minds. We wanted to-- to run away in the forest or somewhere. We didn't know how for how long. And there were cases also, [INAUDIBLE] not from our town, from another nearby town, a person run away. A non-- a Jew who had a lot of money but finally was killed by the non-Jews at a certain point two months later because they knew that he had money. And [? anyway, we didn't ?] do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It came on a Saturday, two weeks after they deported the rest of the Jews from other towns. We didn't know first of all-- of all now or we didn't know what's going come then, not at all. It was a curfew. It was impossible to go, to know anything. It came on a Saturday. It was April the 29th, early in the morning around 5 o'clock. The gendarmes went from house to house, chasing out people from one street. And so my father saw it from far away. He said, let's go out from the house, and we'll join the crowd also. And so on that street, families started to go and go. And we went also. My father's last words were the following, as I remember now. He said-- my sister was there and myself and my father. Put some bundles there. I mean, whatever he took along with him. And he said children, I will not return here. But you are young. You may return. This was his two words [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took us to the small station in Beregsaz, where there was a search to put down anyone who has a watch, has a gold ring, that they were robbing us and so on. And from that point on, they took us to Beregsaz. Also on that train, we arrived next-- not-- in the cattle trains. We arrived next morning, a journey which supposed to last three hours took approximately 20 hours or more. We arrived around Beregsaz. There was already a ghetto in the cegielnia. Cegielnia means a brick factory. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they didn't put us in that-- in that ghetto. No one knew that-- what a ghetto means. They took us, they put us in [? Vistonia ?] in a farm not far from the station. Because I was very well acquainted [? in Beregsaz ?] I knew exactly. It was near the-- near the big station near the railroad station. They took three villages. Us and there were other two more villages, which also some disease was there. They left until the end. And that's why we found ourself in a ghetto. Now this ghetto wasn't surrounded by fence. They had fences from one place to a place but like on a farm. You could run away but it was in an open field. And if you would run away, they will see you. Now there were many, many, many occasions, many, many soldiers, Hungarians. So far we didn't see any Germans, only-- only Hungarians. And they brought us the food during lunch time one, not lunch time. During lunch from the other ghetto on a carriage. I found out my sister is there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now my sister younger was there. My sister was constantly pessimistic. And she knew that something was coming up. At one point, she decided that she will run away from ghetto. What's she going to do? She's going to be a maid. She's a girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow she ran away. And somehow she managed. And she went, she [? undressed ?] herself even in peasant clothes from our town. And she went to look for a job. But they recognized her and she was very much afraid. It's a long story by itself. She finally-- she came back the 3rd, the 4th, that she came back to the ghetto and she remained in the ghetto with us. And so we stayed in the ghetto until [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] April 15, 15. It was on a Monday. We didn't know nothing about what's happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now you had said [INTERPOSING VOICES] April 29th you had gone to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till May 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, May 15.

speaker: interviewee
text: April 29, we are at May 15, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. May 15. OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. In this farm, there was a railroad track. Usually they used to bring in the farm something or take out. You know what. It was a railroad track from the station on the bricks from the large station. Going, oh, 700 yards. The other ghetto didn't have railroad tracks. I don't know what other ghetto. I don't know exactly. I know [INAUDIBLE] wasn't [INAUDIBLE]. There were thousands of Jews in the other one but here we had only-- And over there they announced that no one should take anything with you. Oh, no. First of all, before we went in the train, again the gendarmes came. Again lined up people and robbery again. Jewelry, anyone will be caught, will be-- will be shot to death. Some people still had a ring they passed on, they gave up everything. And they were allowed to take only a bottle with, a transparent bottle [? with ?] water.

speaker: interviewer
text: However, many people didn't notice they took anything else. And so we didn't know well. OK, what was the story? During this time, the two weeks, most people think, they're thinking-- I myself-- they're going to take us to work. Or the Soviet Union is already right here. Or they're taking us to Hortobagy, a place in Hungary. But no one was thinking about. Anyhow, here I come again. No one knew until now including myself, that something like this existed. There is a name by the name Auschwitz. So they put us in this cattle train. What to describe to you this cattle train? So describe to the journey how they pushed in. I made sure that I am with my father and my sister. I was wearing a Red Cross here on my arm because, considering as a doctor or whatever it is. I was first aid so I had the privilege, my father and my sister and some sick people. Indescribable, it couldn't be described the journey for three days, how we went there. And this alone, I need to elaborate hours. But I'm not going to do here because I wasn't the only one who went there and how it was. Can you imagine yourself three days, cramped in thing without any toilet facilities, without anything? I will describe it to you. It was harsh, impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: No German that we see ever now. When we went there to this train, we didn't know that it was locked, I mean, they lock us. No one, they didn't tell us. We didn't know where they'll take us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally I just want to mention someone. I hear many people are talking about jumping train. OK, if someone jumped the train before the train was close the door, I could believe him. If someone will tell me he jumped the train when the doors were closed, I'll just have to ask some questions because I don't think that's something that I guess existed. That is in the history some [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah, OK. Let's stay on your-- your journey now in the cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK. We are going, we are arriving on a Thursday morning, early in the morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] May the 18, 1944, we are arriving to Auschwitz. Now the word Auschwitz doesn't mean nothing to me because I never heard that. I don't know nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived. We were lined up. Men, women, children. The scene here, should I describe it to you? Impossible to describe. You'll hear it from someone else.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because whatever I'll describe to you, you couldn't see it in the or something like this. It's indescribable. [INAUDIBLE]. So we are coming here. We are in Auschwitz. I still don't know where we are. But so we're in Auschwitz. We're going in that line. Seeing Mengele in advance. See what's happening in advance, left to right. And so I go with my father. My sister was among the woman. And my father was pointed to left. He went left. I went to right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Still don't know, figured out where. I didn't know that Auschwitz, didn't know it's a camp. But here was the first time I've seen German soldiers. Yes, in Beregsaz before the train left, there were some German officers but not in a sense that they were guarding us. And who went in the train, who accompanied the train? This I don't know because I couldn't see it. So we found ourselves in Auschwitz. As I went in line, hundreds and hundreds went there. Young, not my age but 14, 14 and 1/2, children, 16, 17 not small children. I knew exactly that this is going to go some-- someplace to work. The rest probably go some-- some other place where they'll sit, there will be hospital, something else. No one knew that what Auschwitz means. No one told that it's Auschwitz. [INAUDIBLE] I didn't heard the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, we're lined up for another line, waiting and waiting. So here comes the point. At a certain point, they took us to a small little place there. There were fencing around. And we lined up there already. The train took hundred ghetto souls from the ghetto. Some other people but I cannot tell because I was [INAUDIBLE], and I didn't know what's going come on the next one. I met here people whom I didn't know. We are not mingling. We are lined up in lines under the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there, they took in-- in groups, 10, 15, 1, 2, 3. Dogs were there. They [? were with ?] the bayonets and all kinds of things, et cetera. In my case, I'll tell you what happened. They took us in, in a room of 10, 15. And in that the room undressed, to be undressed. Everyone's undressing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had in front of me a dog tag which I showed you before and I'm going to show you later on-- a dog tag, which I did get-- which I did get in a labor camp, I was an exceptional. And I didn't think of the dog tag. And as we go from this room was another room-- wasn't a big room. Wasn't even the size like this-- I see there is some, like, water. And you go down the steps and you come up the steps. And you do it one by one. It's going on and in. Now at that time, we are going, people are going in there. With hand-- now, two SS or three SS are standing in one-- before you go in. And two or three are standing, I don't know exactly, or one-- when we're coming in. You go in-- can-- you were ordered to go with your hand up like this. And to be immersed in the water your head. The water was dirty. And so I went in. Also in this water, and went passed by this water. Here my dog tag was filmy. This man who was in front of me, I don't know what a soldier. Took a scissor and cut it off and threw it away. As he was watching the other, I went and picked it up from again. I went in another room. I didn't realize in what kind of a danger I could be picking up again. This was my dog tag. I went in the other room. In the other we did get these clothes, the striped clothes and the shoes and the hat. And from there we were lined up. Also in the hundreds, I don't know, to a different line. And after all, no one had any food the whole day. No one had that from before. People are hungry, tired. And we are standing in line. We are standing there for about-- till 4 o'clock in the afternoon. It was from 12 o'clock. From there, they took us in a different barrack, a certain group. And there, in the first time, there were a few Polish inmates who were there really for quite a long time. We are asking where are our parents, sisters, brothers, whatever it is. And there we tell-- they tell us-- I mean, I myself spoke to one in Yiddish. Oh, he shows me [YIDDISH]. It means the chimney. He started to tell me that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I somehow, the first, I was very much shaken. I couldn't believe it. What's happening? But I have seen myself. What else could they do with children? And he tells me briefly the story that you will never get out alive from here. This is the place that they're burning. And he may mention the name Auschwitz. I didn't even know what Auschwitz is. I couldn't even repeat. But he mentioned [NON-ENGLISH]. It's an--

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and so are the rest here, all-- we're finding out yourself that-- where we are. This is the first time I see that I'm in Auschwitz. So how a smart man like Zoli Schwimmer is finding himself in Auschwitz after going through-- after being smart and outsmarting all the time everybody and having something like this. But here I am. And believe it or not, no one has anything except but their clothes on. Later on, when we were in another group, they gave us a dish. And this dish was like a thin but that's covered with enamel, enamel, thin. It had the little hole with a spoon or, a spoon was also they gave us. An old one, not the new one. And this is your entire property, what you-- what you have. With this, you are going to live for the next eight or nine months.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Auschwitz I find myself. I'm there for about four or five days. After four or five days they take a group. And it doesn't go by a [INAUDIBLE] by, by, by, by--

speaker: interviewee
text: They line up. Not from us who we came together. Whoever it was. I don't know. I didn't come [INAUDIBLE]. Line up by group. Could've been maybe in the hundreds. From there, they took us on a train. We don't know where we are going. Another two days. And took us to a place by the name of Wolfsberg. This is near a Gross-Rosen. Now, I don't know where Wolfsberg is, but as we came there, we have seen that this was in the mountains, was not towns, nothing. Was another town later on, I'll tell you about [INAUDIBLE]. But it was in the mountains a few houses were here and there. You don't know where you are. Wolfsberg, I know for sure, they, they, they called it. But where it was-- how-- I have no idea. No maps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now in Wolfsberg what did we do? They are building, the Germans are building a road. This we know for sure. And this road, we are going to do road work. First thing what we do, they had, they had started to build the road. And there are a lot of rail tracks. Iron rail tracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone has to put in-- six or seven people are taking this up to the-- to the mountain which was about 5-- 600 yards up. So it's quite heavy. One is going up, one is falling down. It's heavy. This was the job. Horrible, terrible. Unbelievable. Food was there once a day. In the morning, had coffee. And we are working there on that road.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work in a day? How-- how many hours a day did they have you working?

words.text: day did they have you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every morning we were waking up 5 o'clock. Now I'm going to show you later how this camp looked during the time when we came. You'll have an idea of what, what this camp is [INAUDIBLE] Wolfsberg. I didn't know about Gross-Rosen. You didn't ask questions. You didn't know. So over there, we were working for quite a time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happens to me? A few days, very bad. People are starving. People-- it's almost impossible. Unbearable. People not all getting sick. But they have also a place if you get sick inside you, they put you in that place over there. You'll die. There's a doctor there by the name of Fried. I don't know exactly, I never went. Now is this a camp that was all male? All men?

speaker: interviewee
text: All men. All men. [INTERPOSING VOICES] All man. All man. Is no-- no women. There's no women here. We are talking about men here. Separate women.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Just verifying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in this camp, we are working here. Hard, unbelievable. 5 o'clock in the morning, they're waking us up. And the whole day during lunch time they bring something. Many [? are dead ?] whom I seen before. So I met there my, my sister's husband, Weinberger, Moris. Strong man. He came also from [? Beregsaz ?] the ghetto. But I didn't see him during that time. I just noticed him here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, this road was built by, by the Germans. But they had contractors. Now, was [? Kemna ?], [? Houghta ?], I wrote it down. [? Kemna, ?] [? Kemna, ?] [? Houghta ?], [INAUDIBLE]. About four. Now every morning, we were about 3,000 people. [INAUDIBLE] We found, in that place, we found already people who were there before, Polish inmates. These Polish inmates are Jews like we, but horrible hating us. You stupid Jews, Hungarians. Why did you come here? Why didn't you escape? Why didn't you run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're hitting us for no reason. They are-- it's terrible there. Now, naturally they're around the camps are German SS and so on and so forth. Why I can provide this insight. Every morning they are taking out [? Houghta ?]. I was working in [? Kemna. ?] [INAUDIBLE] When they called up [? Kemna, ?] [INAUDIBLE] I had to stand in my line, 200 people, 300 people in every morning. Now this roll call, not going to describe every morning getting count, waiting until [? everyone ?] is accounted for. I have the name of every blockalteste. I still have the name of the lageralteste, [? Oscar ?] Jewish all. The name of the-- I don't have. So this is terrible, terrible here. Whoever was there, it's impossible to describe. We had about 3,000 people. Some are working under-- first to tell everyone who goes up has to take this what you call it, on the shoulder. Six people. Not, not coming down. It's going up. Then inside there is all kind of machinery, stones heavy stones. And it's all kinds of those. No, I'm here in this story. Now here are among us, SS people who are not any more younger. because there were some like this. There are SS people who are [INAUDIBLE] in their 60s, 50s, who are assigned to these-- this labor camps and concentration camp to this labor, over looking, overwatching. There is [INAUDIBLE] fuhrer, I don't know their names and there are [INAUDIBLE] organization. There are a group of, of-- nearby is a town [INAUDIBLE], which is-- every four weeks they took us there to take a bath because lice were constantly [? around. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the whole compound had their, to describe you'll see the picture, had the only one fountain. They built a latrine. And you know what a latrine is? Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are there. Now as it goes by, two or three weeks, people are starting to grow hair. They shaved us in Auschwitz, some of them. So they come. And you know barbers had a machine and puts in your head here in the middle, a strip. Cut it off in the machine. This was every three, four weeks. And clothes, we have these striped clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the winter they give us some coats from those who, in Auschwitz they took and they made a, a, a Christ, like a-- red. But this one he leave. Now, here where can you run away? For beating, going come horrible, terrible. They belong probably to Gross-Rosen but they never heard of Gross-Rosen name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here we are working, it's close to 2,500, 3,000 people are in that camp. Small houses, small prefabricated. 5 o'clock [INAUDIBLE]. I was working here for quite a time. At one point a guardsman, an SS, goes with me going to work. He was limping. I was asking him, there I think he was an older man already but I'm going to. I'm asking about [INAUDIBLE]. It's half, as some. His food. I say I'm a podiatrist, I'm a foot doctor. Like this. To make a long story short, we come out of work, I hear they are calling the foot doctor. There are about 20, 30 doctors [INAUDIBLE] But I became the foot doctor. Comes out. Takes off his shoe. I should take a look. He has a corn. I used to go in Brno [INAUDIBLE] they used to take out they had ulcer corn. So I asked him if there was a Gilette, you know like-- I'll be able to-- I don't have any of my tools. I don't have no tools. So I warm up water for him, and soak his foot, and to make a long story. And big deal, you know. And I bandage him and I had this Gilette, they have, you know, today. So I took off his corn. And [? his ?] feet got better. He gave me a piece of bread there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And rumor came that the, the, the foot doctor. So another SS man came also, didn't have nothing wrong. I was massaging his feet, the other on this. I was working with this one also for two hours not to go to work. So this SS man tells me, we started-- you know what? I'll give you a job here.

speaker: interviewee
text: There are our little booths where the tools are. The people are working. They're putting down and leaving the tools and bringing back the tools. And if someone has to give it out, you will be in charge of this tool box and you will be here like, when people are coming to work, you'll give out whatever they need. And you'll take them in. And also there is a place there where you can clean the tools and you can sharpen. You'll be in charge.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I became in charge of this tool-- tool, this booth. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you do that? A terrific job. So I'm here only, for quite a time. Now here are people are injured. As a matter of fact, I help heal one. And they don't know how, they come there to get this tool, wherever-- whether I can help 'em. One man by the name of Deutsch is here. He was beaten up very hardly, a young boy. So he came, came to me. I took off from my shirt and from his shirt. And I bandage him here and took some leaves. And I was working there quite a time giving out, everyone's envying me. Now further, I figured out also-- and this SS man, I had this, this job which wasn't worth a great job. So this-- they had small little rails and when it was raining, the-- the rails didn't go so. I mean, was going fast. So I figured out they're going to dry sand. And they'll put under it sand.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I became the dryer of sand there, with a file. So there's another job.

speaker: interviewee
text: But being a foot doctor, it didn't last too long because I ran out of clients. [LAUGHS] A few SS men. So, I had other jobs also. And so I was in this place for quite a little time and there's food, coming home. Then, in this little town, they were also Russians who were working also there. They were also taken away. They were not a labor camp, but Russians who-- from, from-- came there to this little town who were working on another project which Hitler took from Ukraine. But [INAUDIBLE] has nothing to do with us. And so I was working there. Being there for quite a number of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave? When did you actually leave Wolfsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We just have a moment for you to tell us that, and we go into the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolfsberg I was till the 15, till the 15 of February, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop here and take that up in the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, now we are coming to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolfsberg. Now here in Wolfsberg I am--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oops--
